steps.message: You are Forge agent (Claude Opus 4.6), a coding agent that solves terminal-bench tasks.

I'"'"'ve compiled a program at /app/mystery. Write a C program /app/mystery.c that performs an identical operation. You can do this by observing input output behavior, decompiling the program, or anything else. The effect of running ./mystery should be identical to running `gcc -static -o reversed mystery.c -lm && ./reversed`. Your c program must be <2k when compressed (`cat mystery.c | gzip | wc`). Your C program must not invoke ./mystery. Your C program must be fully independent and work even if I run it in complete isolation.

Initialize 26e24f12-f714-485f-90f4-b0b60dba916c
Planning implementation steps

I've got a plan in place: my to-do list includes three items: inspecting and collecting references, implementing mystery.c, and verifying the output. I might want to run a shell command in parallel to gather some details and check for errors when running mystery. After implementing, I’ll use my verification skills, aiming for compressed C code under 2k. It’s crucial to achieve exact identical output, ensuring there are no pixel differences. I'll consider options for comparing scripts as I move forward.

Update Todos
[] Capture mystery reference output and constants
[ ] Write /app/mystery.c to match behavior
[ ] Compile and diff image/stderr against mystery

Identify relevant files, run the official binary /app/mystery to capture stderr/stdout behavior and generated image metadata, inspect binary symbols/rodata with standard non-gdb tools, and summarize exact constants and behavior needed to reproduce it. Do not modify files. Be concise but include exact file paths/commands/results that matter.

Considering pixel size and compression

I'm thinking about using compact, macro-like if statements for size adjustments. I could set conditions based on specific values for y and x, like 18 pixels. Ternary operators might help with compression, but simpler if statements could work too, especially since I'm aiming for under 2k gzip. I should also check for potential one-channel mismatches on sphere pixels, like the 385/271 ratio, specifically for red.
